_notes: Auto-generated by scripts/classify_districts.py (Stage 4) produced an EMPTY rule set: the pack's scraped breadcrumbs are only 2 levels deep (chapter 'Chapter 106 - ZONING' -> article, e.g. 'ARTICLE II. - DISTRICT REGULATIONS'), with no division-level breakdown, so Stage 4's article-level grouping lumped all 12 base zoning districts (AG, RSF, RMF, MHP, RB, DBD, TBD, CBD, HBD, BCD, LM, HM -- 36 sections) plus the Cluster Housing Overlay, College/University District, Floodplain Overlay, Planned Unit District, Urban Forest Overlay, and Industrial Park Overlay (26 more sections, 62 total for the article) into one heterogeneous 'DISTRICT REGULATIONS' bucket and correctly refused to assign it a single district. Human-reviewed 2026-07-18: like lynchburg-va (not fredericksburg-va or manassas-va), Salem subdivides EACH base district into 3 separate sections sharing a district-scoped section-number prefix (Sec. 106-XXX.1 Statement of intent, .2 Permitted uses, .3 Site development regulations) whose titles never repeat the district code -- e.g. 'Sec. 106-202.2. - Permitted uses.' does not say 'RSF' anywhere -- so a title_contains rule keyed on the district name/code would only catch the purpose/uses/standards sections for whichever ONE the name happens to appear in (none) and miss every section. So this pack uses the same numeric-prefix keying strategy as lynchburg-va: article_contains='DISTRICT REGULATIONS' (breadcrumb[1], read from source.metadata['breadcrumb'] at runtime per app/source_classifier.py -- this pack's top-level manifest 'article'/'division' fields are always blank, unlike other packs, so the classifier's article/division scoping ONLY works because it reads metadata.breadcrumb, not the top-level fields) plus title_contains='106-XXX.' per district, where XXX is the district's own 3-section number prefix confirmed by reading each cluster's 'Statement of intent' full_text. District codes and mapping rationale, confirmed against both the ordinance text and the city's live ArcGIS zoning services (Zoning FeatureServer at services7.arcgis.com/RL8aZgZHmJOdyNXk/arcgis/rest/services/Zoning/FeatureServer/0, field ZONING/DistrictName, which lists the exact same 12 base-district codes AG/RSF/RMF/MHP/RB/DBD/TBD/CBD/HBD/BCD/LM/HM plus COL/CUD/PUD, confirming these are the city's real, currently-mapped zoning categories): (1) Sec. 106-200.x AG Agricultural District ('created in recognition that some land exists within the city that continues to be used for limited agricultural purposes') -> vocabulary's dedicated 'agricultural' term, matching the manassas-va A-1 precedent (named 'A' code with agricultural-activity purpose text, unlike lynchburg's R-C which stayed residential-low-density for lacking both). (2) Sec. 106-202.x RSF Residential Single Family District and Sec. 106-204.x RMF Residential Multi-Family District and Sec. 106-206.x MHP Manufactured Home Park District -> all three collapse to 'residential-low-density' (the closed DISTRICT_VOCAB has no separate low/medium/high-density or manufactured-housing residential tiers, same precedent as every prior pack); MHP is a judgment call the same shape as lynchburg's R-C -- its purpose text is purely about manufactured-housing community standards with no commercial content, so it stays residential rather than unmapped. (3) Sec. 106-208.x RB Residential Business District, Sec. 106-210.x DBD Downtown Business District, Sec. 106-212.x TBD Transitional Business District, and Sec. 106-213.x CBD Community Business District all map to 'mixed-use-core' -- this is FOUR districts in one vocabulary bucket, more than any prior pack, and is a deliberate reading of the actual permitted-uses text, not just the purpose paragraphs: RB has real by-right Single Family Dwelling and Townhouse uses alongside commercial (genuinely mixed); DBD, TBD, and CBD each have 'Mixed Use' as a permitted-by-right Miscellaneous Use Type AND a full slate of residential dwelling types (Multi-Family, Single-Family Attached/Detached, Two-Family, Townhouse) permitted by special exception, with intent text explicitly invoking 'upper floor residential uses are encouraged' (DBD/TBD) or 'coexist with residences in a transitional, mixed-use environment' (CBD) -- these read as a graduated downtown-to-corridor mixed-use sequence (RB -> DBD -> TBD -> CBD), all qualitatively different from the auto-oriented general commercial that follows. (4) Sec. 106-214.x HBD Highway Business District -> 'commercial-employment': unlike RB/DBD/TBD/CBD, HBD's permitted-uses list has NO 'Mixed Use' type and NO residential uses even by special exception (explicitly 'Residential Use Types (None)' in its special-exception table) -- auto-oriented general commercial along arterials, not mixed-use. (5) Sec. 106-216.x BCD Business Commerce District -> 'commercial-employment', a JUDGMENT CALL: purpose text calls it 'a flexible district to encourage the orderly development of commercial and light industrial uses' and its permitted-uses list includes 'Custom Manufacturing Industry Type I' alongside offices/labs/commercial -- closer to an employment/flex district than either mixed-use or heavy industrial, and DISTRICT_VOCAB's 'commercial-employment' term is the closest fit (site development standards aren't even fixed in the ordinance -- they're set case-by-case at rezoning, defaulting to HBD's standards if unset, reinforcing that BCD behaves as a commercial/employment variant rather than a distinct industrial category). (6) Sec. 106-218.x LM Light Manufacturing District and Sec. 106-220.x HM Heavy Manufacturing District -> 'industrial-zone' (unambiguous: both purpose texts explicitly describe industrial/warehouse uses with increasing intensity/outdoor-storage tolerance from LM to HM). (7) Sec. 106-224.x CUD College and University District -> 'commercial-employment', a SECOND JUDGMENT CALL following the lynchburg-va IN-1/IN-2 precedent exactly: purpose text describes 'classroom, office, laboratory, and residential areas, as well as areas devoted to culture, recreation and food service' in 'a campus environment' -- an institutional employment/services center, not a zero-employee use, and DISTRICT_VOCAB has no separate institutional tier; unlike PUD (below), CUD DOES have a real fixed by-right use list (Educational Facilities, College/University) the same as every other base district, so it behaves like a normal base district despite the flexible campus framing -- flagged as lower-confidence than the unambiguous mappings since (like lynchburg's IN-1/IN-2) it lacks a GIS-verified parcel fixture in this batch. Left unmapped (unknown/general), matching the 'don't force overlay/floating-zone articles into the vocab' precedent from every prior pack: Sec. 106-222.x Cluster Housing Overlay (explicitly an overlay reducing lot-size/frontage requirements 'from those required in the underlying single family residential zoning district' -- it modifies an existing base district, it is not one); Sec. 106-226.x Floodplain Overlay District (10 sections: definitions, floodway, elevation certificates, variances -- textbook floodplain overlay, same treatment as every other city's flood regulations); Sec. 106-230.x Urban Forest Overlay District (tree-canopy standards layered on top of base zoning, explicitly excluding RSF/RMF parcels); Sec. 106-232.x Industrial Park Overlay District (buffering/intensity standards layered on top of base industrial zoning, not itself a base district). Sec. 106-228.x Planned Unit District (PUD) is the HARDEST judgment call in this pack and was deliberately left UNMAPPED despite being a real, GIS-mapped zoning category (the live ArcGIS Zoning layer does have PUD-coded parcels): its own intent text says applications 'may propose any residential, civic, and/or commercial use type,' and Sec. 106-228.2 confirms 'No use type may be allowed within the planned unit district unless approved by Council as part of the final master plan' -- i.e. PUD has NO fixed by-right or special-exception use list in the ordinance text at all; the actual entitlements for any given PUD parcel live in a separately-approved master plan document that is not part of this source pack. Mapping PUD to any single vocabulary term would let the orchestrator synthesize a likely_allowed/restricted conclusion the ordinance text itself cannot support -- violating the 'no citations, no conclusion' invariant in spirit even when a PUD section IS retrieved, since that section describes a process, not a use determination. This is a deliberately more conservative call than CUD's, which has a real fixed use list. ARTICLE III. - USE AND DESIGN STANDARDS (Sec. 106-300 onward: agriculture, accessory apartment, home occupations, dwelling-type standards, commercial/industrial use standards, towers, chickens, etc.) is left entirely unmapped as cross-cutting use-specific standards applicable across multiple districts, matching how every prior pack (fredericksburg, lynchburg, manassas, henrico) left their equivalent use-standards articles unmapped. Rules are first-match.
